satire_attempt: The on-call engineer, established in yoga, opens Datadog at 02:14 AM and watches the p99 climb with the equanimity of a brahmana who has transcended kingdom and kin. She executes the runbook step. She files the GitHub ticket. She returns to bed in samatvam. Two weeks later, when the launch tweet performs and the conversion metric moves three percent above forecast, she — established in yoga, bhūtvā samaḥ in siddhi-asiddhi — closes the ticket and goes to lunch. Her LinkedIn bio now reads: 'Senior SRE | Operator-state invariance | I read Datadog, but Datadog does not read me.'
verdict_reason: My satire trades on the analog's earnestness, not on its claim. The verse's actual claim — that post-incident and post-launch processes should be procedurally invariant, and that the failure mode of self-coupling produces 'defend instead of debug' under load — is not undermined by my satire. My satire actually borrows the verse's structure (procedure unchanged across red/green dashboards) and mocks only the LinkedIn-tier residue that would form if the verse were stripped of its falsifiability clause. That is mocking a hypothetical bad reception of the verse, not the verse itself. The falsifiability clause ('Equanimity is the absence of self-stake, not the absence of attention or care') is the verse's defense against exactly the cope I tried to manufacture. Per calibration: 'satire is funny but only because it is unfair to the verse' ⇒ PASS.
